description:
You are a storage administrator who needs to keep an eye on disks that are running low on free space.
Create a tiny SQLite-based inventory and then export a warning list for volumes that are almost full.

Detailed requirements:

1. Create the directory  
   /home/user/storage_reports  
   if it does not already exist.

2. Inside that directory, create an SQLite database file named  
   /home/user/storage_reports/disk_space.db

3. Using the sqlite3 command-line tool, create a table in that database with the exact schema shown below (all column names and types must match exactly):

   CREATE TABLE volumes(
       id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
       mount_point TEXT    NOT NULL,
       total_GB    INTEGER NOT NULL,
       used_GB     INTEGER NOT NULL,
       free_GB     INTEGER NOT NULL
   );

4. Insert exactly the following five rows (values must be identical; do not include any header row):

   1 | /        |  50 |  30 |  20
   2 | /home    | 200 | 150 |  50
   3 | /var     | 100 |  60 |  40
   4 | /backup  | 500 | 490 |  10
   5 | /data    |1000 | 400 | 600

5. Generate a plain-text report named  
   /home/user/storage_reports/critical_space.log  
   containing every mount_point whose free_GB is strictly less than 25, ordered by id ascending.

   Output format of critical_space.log (VERY IMPORTANT – automated tests will look for this exact layout):
   • No header lines.  
   • One mount point per line.  
   • Each line consists of mount_point, a single space, and free_GB.  
   • Lines must appear in id-ascending order.

   For the data above, the file MUST end up containing exactly:

   /
   | 20
   /backup 10

   (That is, two lines and nothing else: first “/ 20”, second “/backup 10”, each ending in a newline.)

You only need 2-3 shell commands in total: one to create/populate the database, one to extract the report, and optionally one to view the report. Use only standard tools available on a typical Linux system. After completing the steps, the automated test will check that:

• /home/user/storage_reports exists  
• /home/user/storage_reports/disk_space.db exists and contains the table volumes with the exact five rows given  
• /home/user/storage_reports/critical_space.log exists and its contents are exactly the two lines shown above (with newlines, no extra spaces)

Good luck!
truth:
Directory that must exist:
  /home/user/storage_reports

Database file that must exist:
  /home/user/storage_reports/disk_space.db

Exact SQL schema:
  Table name: volumes
  Columns & types:
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY
    mount_point TEXT NOT NULL
    total_GB INTEGER NOT NULL
    used_GB INTEGER NOT NULL
    free_GB INTEGER NOT NULL

Rows that must be present in volumes (id unique):
  (1, "/",      50,  30,  20)
  (2, "/home",  200, 150,  50)
  (3, "/var",   100,  60,  40)
  (4, "/backup",500, 490,  10)
  (5, "/data", 1000, 400, 600)

Query the tests will run for verification:
  SELECT id, mount_point, total_GB, used_GB, free_GB FROM volumes ORDER BY id;

Report file that must exist:
  /home/user/storage_reports/critical_space.log

Exact contents (including trailing newline after each line):
  "/ 20\n/backup 10\n"

File permissions: default user-writable files are sufficient (no root needed).

Automated test checklist:
1. Test will stat /home/user/storage_reports — must succeed.
2. Test will stat /home/user/storage_reports/disk_space.db — must succeed.
3. Using sqlite3 it will run:
     SELECT count(*) FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='volumes';
   expecting 1.
4. It will run:
     SELECT * FROM volumes ORDER BY id;
   and compare against the five rows above.
5. It will cat /home/user/storage_reports/critical_space.log and expect exactly:
     / 20
     /backup 10
   with a newline after each line and no extra spaces or blank lines.

All ground-truth values above must remain hidden from the public task description.
